beats.title: Invocation and Beginning of Garuda Purana
beats.summary: This is the beginning of the Garuda Purana, starting with the Karma Kanda section. It includes invocations to Ganesha, Saraswati, Narayana, Nara, and the supreme being, followed by a declaration of victory.

beats.title: The Origin and Avatars of Nārāyaṇa
beats.summary: The chapter begins with the sages in Naimiṣāraṇya asking Sūta about the supreme deity, creation, avatars, and dharma. Sūta recounts the Garuḍa Purāṇa as told by Vyāsa, describing Nārāyaṇa as the supreme lord and listing his 24 avatars from Kumāra to Kalki.

beats.title: Transmission of the Garuda Purana
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana recounts the transmission of the Purana from Brahma to Vyasa, and from Vyasa to Suta. It describes how Brahma, along with other sages, approached Shiva on Mount Kailasa to learn the essence of scriptures. Shiva reveals that he meditates on Vishnu as the supreme being, and then leads the group to Vishnu in Shvetadvipa. Vishnu declares himself as the supreme deity, the source of all creation, and the essence of all practices and scriptures. The chapter also mentions Garuda's penance to Vishnu.

beats.title: Origin of the Garuda Purana
beats.summary: Garuda asks Vishnu to free his mother Vinata from serpent slavery by bringing the nectar. Vishnu grants this, declares Garuda his vehicle, and promises the Garuda Purana will be revealed. Garuda then teaches the Garuda Purana to Kashyapa, who uses it to revive a burnt tree.

beats.title: Origin and Scope of the Garuda Purana
beats.summary: Suta narrates the origin and scope of the Garuda Purana, describing how Garuda became Vishnu's vehicle and the purana's contents, including creation, cosmology, duties, and liberation.

beats.title: The Nine Creations and the Origin of Beings
beats.summary: Rudra asks Janardana to explain creation, secondary creation, dynasties, Manvantaras, and genealogies. Hari (Vishnu) describes the nine types of creation: primary (Mahat, tanmatras, vaikarika), secondary (mukhya, tiryaksrotas, urdhvasrotas, arvaksrotas, anugraha), and the ninth Kaumara creation. He explains how Brahma creates beings from his body, including devas, asuras, pitris, humans, and various creatures, and the origin of the four varnas and their respective lokas.

beats.title: Chapter 5
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of various beings by Brahma, including sages, deities, and other entities. It details the lineage of Daksha, his daughters, and their marriages to sages and gods, leading to the birth of many beings. The chapter also recounts the story of Sati, her marriage to Rudra, and the subsequent destruction of Daksha's sacrifice.

beats.title: Genealogies of Daityas and Birds
beats.summary: This chapter lists various demons (daityas) born in the lineage of Prahlada, including Vyaṃśa, Śalya, Nabhas, Vātāpi, Namuci, Ilvala, Khasṛman, Añja, Naraka, Kālanābha, and the Nivātakavacas. It also describes the six sons of Tāmrā: Śukī, Śyenī, Bhāsī, Sughrīvī, Śucigṛdhrikā, and their offspring, which include various birds like parrots, owls, hawks, vultures, etc.

beats.title: Progeny of the Female Birds and Animals
beats.summary: This chapter describes the progeny of various female birds and animals, including śyenī, bhāsī, gṛdhrī, and sugrīvī, who gave birth to different species.

beats.title: Genealogy of Beings and the Maruts
beats.summary: This chapter lists the progeny of various beings, including Vinata's sons Garuda and Aruna, Surasa's thousand serpents, Kadru's thousand hooded serpents, and the offspring of Krodha, Surabhi, Ira, Khaga, and Aristha. It also enumerates the forty-nine Maruts and describes them as forms of Hari, worshipped by kings, demons, and gods.

beats.title: Ritual Worship of Sūrya and Other Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides detailed instructions for the ritual worship (pūjā) of Sūrya (the Sun) and other deities, including the nine planets (Navagraha), Śiva, Viṣṇu, and Sarasvatī. It includes specific mantras for each deity, the sequence of offerings (āsana, avāhana, pādya, arghya, ācamana, snāna, vastra, upavīta, gandha, puṣpa, dhūpa, dīpa, namaskāra, pradakṣiṇa, visarjana), and the nyāsa (placement) of mantras on the body. The chapter emphasizes the spiritual benefits of these rituals for achieving dharma, kāma, artha, and mokṣa.

beats.title: Vajranābha Maṇḍala Construction and Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the construction and ritual use of the Vajranābha maṇḍala, a geometric diagram for worshiping Viṣṇu. It details the drawing of the maṇḍala with sixteen compartments, placement of deities (Viṣṇu, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Brahmā, Śrīdhara, and Indra and others in the directions), color assignments, and the nyāsa (placement) of these deities on the worshiper's body. The chapter concludes that by performing this worship with offerings like sandalwood paste, one attains the supreme abode.

beats.title: The Rite of Viṣṇu Initiation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ritual of initiation (dīkṣā) for a disciple, including homa (fire offerings) with specific numbers of oblations, the visualization of elements, the construction of a lotus maṇḍala, and the placement of the guru's hand on the disciple's head. It also covers naming the disciple and mentions the sin-destroying power of the guru's touch.

beats.title: Worship of Mahalakshmi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of Mahalakshmi, including the construction of a lotus-shaped mandala, placement of deities in directions and corners, and the recitation of specific mantras for Lakshmi and Saraswati. It also mentions the worship of Durgā, Gaṇa, Guru, and Kṣetrapāla, and concludes with a homa (fire sacrifice).

beats.title: Navavyūhārcana: The Ninefold Worship of Viṣṇu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ritual of Navavyūhārcana, a nine-fold worship of Viṣṇu involving meditation, mantra nyāsa, and mudrās. It details the process of purifying the body, visualizing the deity, and performing the worship with specific mantras and hand gestures.

beats.title: Pūjānukrama: The Order of Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a detailed ritual procedure for worship (pūjā) and fire sacrifice (homa), including mantras, nyāsa, offerings, and meditation on the supreme being. It describes the worship of Viṣṇu in his various forms and the protective power of the Dvārakācakra.

beats.title: Viṣṇu Pañjara Hymn
beats.summary: This chapter presents the Viṣṇu Pañjara (Viṣṇu's Cage) hymn, a protective prayer where the devotee invokes Viṣṇu in various forms and weapons to guard all directions. It recounts how this hymn was previously taught to Śiva and used by Kātyāyanī to defeat demons like Mahiṣāsura and Raktabīja. The chapter concludes by stating that reciting this hymn with devotion leads to victory over enemies.

beats.title: Dhyanayoga: The Yoga of Meditation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, titled 'Dhyanayoga' (Yoga of Meditation), presents a discourse on the nature of the supreme reality, Vishnu, who is described as beyond the six modifications (birth, existence, growth, change, decay, and death), beyond the body, mind, intellect, and senses. The text teaches that through meditation on this formless, all-pervading, blissful self, one attains liberation and the supreme abode of Vishnu.

beats.title: The Thousand Names of Vishnu
beats.summary: Rudra asks Vishnu about the supreme chant that liberates from the terrible ocean of samsara. Vishnu responds that praising Vishnu with the thousand names brings liberation, and proceeds to enumerate many names of Vishnu, describing his attributes and his lordship over various beings and elements.

beats.title: The Universal Cause: Hari as the Source of All
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana enumerates the various causes (kāraṇa) of all beings and elements, identifying them with the supreme being Hari (Vishnu/Krishna). It lists numerous names and forms of the divine, including avatars like Matsya, Paraśurāma, and Rāma, and describes the supreme as beyond all sensory attributes and modifications.

beats.title: Chapter 15 (part 3)
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn listing the thousand names of Vishnu (Vishnu Sahasranama), describing his various attributes, forms, and incarnations. It emphasizes his omnipresence, role as the inner controller of all senses and actions, and his manifestations in various beings and concepts.

beats.title: Rudra's Inquiry on Meditation
beats.summary: Rudra asks Vishnu to explain the meditation on the holder of conch, discus, and mace. This chapter is part of the Vishnu Sahasranama discussion in the Garuda Purana.

beats.title: Meditation on Hari and Worship of Surya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the meditation on Vishnu (Hari) and the worship of the Sun (Surya). It begins with a description of the supreme, attributeless Brahman, then provides a mantra and ritual for Surya worship, including the nyasa and offering of arghya.

beats.title: Sūryārcana Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of Sun worship (Suryarcana), including drawing an eight-petalled lotus, invoking the Sun, placing the Sun's limbs in various directions, and worshipping the twelve forms of the Sun along with other deities like Indra, the goddesses Jayā, Vijayā, Jayantī, Aparājitā, and the serpents Śeṣa and Vāsuki.

beats.title: The Worship of Mrityunjaya (Amritesha)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the worship of Mrityunjaya (Amritesha), including the mantra, meditation, and ritual procedures for overcoming death, disease, and enemies.

beats.title: Prāṇeśvaravidyā: The Science of Snakebite Antidotes
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, titled 'Prāṇeśvaravidyā' (the science of the lord of life), deals with snakebite antidotes, mantras, and rituals. It describes fatal bite locations, planetary associations with serpents, and methods for neutralizing poison through mantra recitation, herbal remedies, and meditation on Garuda.

beats.title: Mantras for Protection and Destruction
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various mantras and rituals for protection against snakes, poisons, demons, and natural calamities. It includes instructions for using weapons like the trident, discus, and vajra in conjunction with mantras, as well as methods for consecrating pegs (kilaka) to safeguard fields. The mantras are attributed to Shiva and Garuda, and the chapter emphasizes the power of these mantras to destroy enemies and ward off evil.

beats.title: The Worship of the Five-Faced Śiva
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of worshipping the five-faced (pañcavaktra) form of Śiva, detailing the mantras and kalās (energies) associated with each face: Sadyojāta, Vāmadeva, Aghora, Tatpuruṣa, and Īśāna. The worship is said to grant both enjoyment (bhukti) and liberation (mukti).

beats.title: Śivārcana Prakāra (The Method of Worshiping Shiva)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the procedure for worshiping Shiva (Śivārcana), including mantra recitation, mudras, nyasa, homa (fire sacrifice), and initiation (dīkṣā). It emphasizes that through proper ritual purification, one attains Śivatva (oneness with Shiva).

beats.title: Śiva Pūjā Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides detailed instructions for the worship of Shiva (Śiva), including mantras, rituals, and meditative practices. It covers the use of sacred syllables, the construction of a lotus seat, the invocation of deities and guardians, and the performance of offerings. The text emphasizes the identity of the worshipper with Shiva and the ultimate goal of liberation through knowledge.

beats.title: Shivarcana Nirupana (Description of Shiva Worship)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the meditation and worship of Sadashiva, including his iconographic details, the mantras used, and the benefits of performing the worship with specific time durations. It also mentions that proper worship can prevent untimely death.

beats.title: Worship of Ganesha, Durga, Tripura, and Associated Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the worship of various deities including Ganesha, Durga, Tripura, the Matrikas (Brahmani, Maheshvari, Kaumari, Vaishnavi, Varahi, Indrani, Chamunda, Chandika), the eight Bhairavas, and associated practices such as mantra recitation, yantra meditation, and homa. It provides specific mantras, seating arrangements, and offerings for these rituals.

beats.title: Asana Puja and Mantra Invocation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes a series of mantra invocations and puja rituals, focusing on the worship of the Ananta Shakti Paduka and various other divine padukas (footwear). It includes the recitation of bija mantras, the establishment of the asana (seat), and the beginning of karanyasa (hand gestures) with specific mudras and nyasa for the fingers.

beats.title: Dehanyāsa and Mandala Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 26) describes a ritual practice called dehanyāsa (body placement) and the worship of various mandalas (circles). It includes mantras and offerings to deities such as Kubjikā, Aghorāmukhi, and others, along with instructions for hand gestures and meditation on different mandalas representing elements like wind, moon, and the guru lineage.

beats.title: Mantra for Protection and Destruction
beats.summary: This chapter contains a tantric mantra invoking various deities and powers for protection, destruction of enemies, and removal of poison. It includes references to Rudra, Skanda, and other beings.

beats.title: Gopala Puja Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual worship of Gopala (Krishna) as a means to attain both worldly enjoyment and liberation. It details the placement of deities and guardians at the gates and directions of the sacred space, the worship of Vishnu and his manifestations, the arrangement of the chakra and weapons, and the queens of Krishna. The chapter concludes by stating that all desires are fulfilled through japa, meditation, and worship.

beats.title: Trailokyamohinī (Śrīdhara) Worship Procedure
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a set of mantras and a worship procedure for the deity Trailokyamohinī (Śrīdhara), a form of Viṣṇu. The mantras are intended to attract, subjugate, and fulfill desires, and the worship includes offerings of weapons and attendants like Lakṣmī and Garuḍa.

beats.title: Śrīdhara Worship Ritual
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the detailed ritual for worshipping Lord Vishnu in his form as Śrīdhara, including mantras for each step, the deities to be invoked, and the benefits of performing the worship with devotion.

beats.title: Ritual Worship of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana details the ritual worship of Vishnu, including the mantra, nyasa, meditation, and offerings. It is addressed to Shiva (Vrishabhadhvaja) and emphasizes the purifying and liberating power of the worship.

beats.title: The Worship of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the worship of Vishnu, including hymns of praise, meditation, and the benefits of reciting the sacred text. It emphasizes the importance of devotion to Vishnu for attaining liberation and the Vishnu realm.

beats.title: The Worship of the Five Tattvas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the worship of the five forms of Vishnu (Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, and Nārāyaṇa) through the five tattvas. It provides detailed instructions on the ritual, including purification, nyāsa, meditation, mantra recitation, and offerings, emphasizing the meditative and purificatory aspects.

beats.title: The Worship of Vishnu through the Five Elements
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the worship of Vishnu through the five elements (pañcatattva) and the benefits of such worship. It includes a prayer to Vishnu as the all-pervading light that dispels darkness, and emphasizes that by performing or hearing this worship, one attains the world of Vishnu.

beats.title: Worship of Sudarshana Chakra
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of Sudarshana Chakra, including the mantra, meditation, and ritual procedure. It states that performing this worship removes all diseases and grants attainment of Vishnu's abode. A hymn (stotra) to Sudarshana is also provided, which destroys all sins and leads to Vishnu's world.

beats.title: Hayagriva Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana details the worship of Hayagriva, a form of Vishnu. It includes the mantra, nyasa, meditation, and step-by-step puja procedures, including offerings to various deities and weapons.

beats.title: Hayagriva Worship Procedure
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of Hayagriva, a form of Vishnu, including a hymn of praise and meditation on the deity. It concludes with the benefits of reciting the worship procedure.

beats.title: Gayatri Nyasa
beats.summary: Lord Hari (Vishnu) explains the nyasa (placement) of the Gayatri mantra, detailing its seer (Viśvāmitra), deity (Savitā), and the placement of its syllables on various body parts, along with color associations. The chapter emphasizes that everything touched or seen becomes purified through the Gayatri.

beats.title: Sandhyāvidhi: The Procedure of Twilight Worship
beats.summary: Lord Hari (Vishnu) instructs Rudra (Shiva) on the procedure of Sandhyā (twilight worship), including prāṇāyāma, mārjana, japa of Gāyatrī mantra, and nyāsa, emphasizing its power to destroy sins and lead to Brahmaloka.

beats.title: Gayatri Kalpa: The Ritual of the Gayatri Mantra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Gayatri Kalpa, a ritual practice centered on the Gayatri mantra. It details the mantra's power to destroy sins and grant both worldly enjoyment and liberation. Instructions are given for japa (recitation) with specific counts, offerings in fire (homa), and worship of the goddess Gayatri under various names. The chapter also includes a verse about the goddess being born on the northern peak and dwelling in mountains, with permission from Brahma.

beats.title: Durgājapapūjābalimantranirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the worship of Goddess Durgā, including mantras, rituals, offerings, and visualizations of her various forms with different numbers of arms and weapons. It provides detailed instructions for japa (chanting), homa (fire sacrifice), and bali (offerings) to attain desires and victory.

beats.title: Surya Puja Vidhi
beats.summary: Rudra asks Vasudeva to explain the worship of Surya, who is a form of Vishnu and grants enjoyment and liberation. Vasudeva describes the detailed procedure for Surya puja, including mantras for door deities, the central deity Prabhuta, the eight Dikpalas (Vimala etc.), the eight rays (Dipta etc.), the Sun's form, nyasa, and worship of planets (Soma, Budha, etc.) and other beings like Rahu and Ketu. The chapter concludes that performing this worship leads to Vishnu's abode.

beats.title: Māheśvarī Pūjā Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the detailed procedure for performing the Māheśvarī Pūjā (worship of Lord Shiva), including mantras, nyāsa, maṇḍala, and offerings. It lists various deities and entities to be invoked and worshipped, such as Gaṇapati, Sarasvatī, Nandin, Mahākāla, Gaṅgā, Lakṣmī, and the eight kalās of Satya, among others. The chapter emphasizes the purifying and sin-destroying nature of this worship.

beats.title: Mantra for Subjugation and Death
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a mantra for subjugation and death, dedicated to the goddess Kālarātri. It includes instructions for its recitation and ritual use, emphasizing that no specific tithi, nakshatra, or fasting is required. The mantra is intended to cause harm to enemies.

beats.title: The Ritual of Offering Sacred Threads to Shiva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ritual of 'pavitrāropaṇa' (offering of sacred threads) to Lord Shiva. It details the preparation of the pavitra (sacred thread), the materials to be used, the mantras for purification, the placement of offerings in different directions, and the procedure for worship including homa and bali. The chapter emphasizes the importance of correct ritual performance to remove obstacles and fulfill vows.

beats.title: The Ritual of Offering Sacred Threads to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ritual of offering sacred threads (pavitra) to Lord Vishnu, which is said to grant both worldly enjoyment and liberation. It narrates the origin of the pavitra from a boon given by Vishnu to a Naga named Vapitra during the deva-asura war, and provides detailed instructions on the types of threads, measurements, mantras, and procedures for the pavitra-aropana ceremony.

beats.title: Meditation on the Supreme Brahman
beats.summary: Lord Hari describes the meditation on Brahman, the supreme reality, as a means to destroy the illusion of Maya. He explains the process of withdrawing the senses and mind, the nature of the self as the chariot-rider, and the practice of yoga including yama, niyama, asana, pranayama, pratyahara, dhyana, dharana, and samadhi. The chapter culminates in the visualization of Vishnu's form and the realization of one's identity with Brahman.

beats.title: Śālagrāma Mūrti Lakṣaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics (lakṣaṇa) of Śālagrāma stones, which are considered manifestations of Viṣṇu. It lists various forms of Viṣṇu (e.g., Keśava, Nārāyaṇa, Mādhava, Govinda) and their associated symbols (śaṅkha, cakra, gadā, padma) as seen in the stones. It also describes the colors, markings, and number of chakras that correspond to different deities, including Brahmā, Maheśvara, and others. The chapter concludes with the benefits of worshipping these stones.

beats.title: Vastu Shastra: The Science of Architecture
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides a detailed exposition on Vastu Shastra, the science of architecture and site planning. It describes the layout of a 64-grid (catuḥṣaṣṭipada) Vastu mandala, listing the deities presiding over each pada, including Īśa, Parjanya, Jayanta, Sūrya, Satya, Bhṛgu, Ākāśa, Vāyu, Pūṣā, Vitatha, Graha, Kṣetra, Yama, Gandharva, Bhṛgu, Rāja, Mṛga, Pitṛgaṇa, Dauvārika, Sugrīva, Puṣpadanta, Gaṇādhipa, Asura, Śeṣa, Pāpa, Roga, Ḍahimukha, Bhallāṭa, Soma, Sarpa, Aditi, and Aditi. It also covers the placement of rooms (e.g., kitchen in the southeast, treasury in the north), door directions and their effects, tree planting, and methods for calculating auspicious dimensions using the birth star (nakshatra) and other factors.

beats.title: Architectural Specifications for Temples
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides detailed architectural guidelines for constructing temples (prāsāda), including measurements for the garbha (sanctum), bhitti (walls), śikhara (spire), jaṅghā (wall section), and śukāṅghri (pillar). It describes five primary temple types (Vairāja, Puṣpaka, Kailāsa, Mālikā, Triviṣṭapa) and their derived forms, along with specifications for maṇḍapas (halls), dvāras (doors), and liṅga measurements. The chapter also discusses the placement of deities, dvārapālas (door guardians), and the importance of proper proportions for spiritual and material benefits.

beats.title: Consecration of a Deity's Image
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides detailed instructions for the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of a deity's image. It covers the selection of the site, construction of a pavilion and altar, placement of pots and banners, invocation of deities and directional guardians, purification rituals, bathing of the image with various substances, and final installation with mantras.

beats.title: Ritual for Consecration of a Deity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana details the ritual for consecrating a deity (pratiṣṭhā), including mantras, homa (fire offerings), placement of the deity, and final offerings. It emphasizes the importance of proper procedure and the role of the ācārya.

beats.title: Varṇāśrama Dharma and Yoga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana outlines the duties and spiritual paths for different varnas and ashramas, culminating in the eight-limbed yoga (ashtanga yoga) and the realization of Brahman. It describes the six duties of Brahmins, the dharma of Kshatriyas and Vaishyas, service for Shudras, and the practices for brahmacharis, grihasthas, vanaprasthas, and sannyasis. It then details yamas, niyamas, asana, pranayama, pratyahara, dhyana, dharana, and samadhi, leading to liberation.

beats.title: Daily Rituals for a Brahmin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides detailed instructions on daily rituals for a Brahmin, emphasizing the importance of morning bath (snana), sandhya worship, Gayatri mantra recitation, and other practices for purification and spiritual progress.

beats.title: Nityakarma and Ashauca: Daily Rituals and Rules of Impurity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana details the daily rituals (nityakarma) and rules of impurity (ashauca) for a dvija (twice-born). It prescribes the procedure for japa, tarpana (libations) to deities, sages, and ancestors, worship of gods like Brahma, Shiva, Surya, and Vishnu, and the five great sacrifices (pañca-mahāyajña). It also discusses the duration of ashauca for different varnas and specific life events.

beats.title: Dānadharmanirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, titled 'Dānadharmanirūpaṇa' (Exposition of the Duty of Charity), is narrated by Brahma. It describes the supreme importance of charity (dāna) as a dharma, detailing different types of dāna (nitya, naimittika, kāmya, vimala), the proper recipients (pātra), and the fruits of giving various items such as land, food, cows, gold, etc. It also prescribes specific rituals for atonement and achieving desires through charity and worship of deities. The chapter emphasizes that charity given with faith and to worthy recipients leads to worldly enjoyment and liberation.

beats.title: Prāyaścitta for Mahāpātakas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana prescribes expiatory rites (prāyaścitta) for the five great sins (mahāpātaka): brahmahatyā (killing a brāhmaṇa), surāpāna (drinking liquor), steya (theft of gold), gurutalpaga (incest with guru's wife), and association with such sinners. It details various penances including living in a forest hut, fasting, performing sacrifices, bathing at sacred sites, and observing vows like cāndrāyaṇa. The chapter also extols the power of devotion to Viṣṇu, the virtue of a pativratā (devoted wife), and the purifying effect of dying in a holy place.

beats.title: The Eight Treasures of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana describes the eight treasures (nidhis) of Vishnu, their characteristics, and the nature of humans marked by them. The treasures are Padma, Mahapadma, Makara, Kacchapa, Mukunda, Nanda, Nila, and Sankha. Each treasure is associated with a specific guna (sattva, rajas, tamas) and influences the behavior and destiny of the person bearing its mark.

beats.title: Priyavrata's Lineage and the Geography of the Seven Continents
beats.summary: This chapter describes the lineage of Priyavrata, the division of the seven continents (dvīpas) and oceans, the geography of Jambūdvīpa including Mount Meru, and the genealogy of kings from Nābhi to Viśvagjyoti.

beats.title: Bhuvanakośavarṇana (Description of the Universe)
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana describes the geography of the earth, including the seven continents (dvīpas), the central continent Ilāvṛta, and the surrounding regions such as Bhadrāśva, Ketumāla, Ramyaka, and Kuru. It lists the major mountains, rivers, and peoples of Bhārata (India), dividing them by direction. The chapter also mentions the seven sons of Medhātithi, the ruler of Plakṣadvīpa.

beats.title: Bhuvanakośa: Description of the Seven Continents
beats.summary: This chapter describes the seven continents (dvīpas) and their rulers, mountains, and rivers, as part of the Bhuvanakośa (cosmography) section of the Garuḍa Purāṇa. It lists the sons of Medhātithi (ruler of Plakṣadvīpa), the sons of Vapuṣmān (ruler of Śālmaladvīpa), the sons of Jyotiṣmān (ruler of Kuśadvīpa), the sons of Dyutimān (ruler of Krauñcadvīpa), the sons of Bhavya (ruler of Śākadvīpa), and the ruler of Puṣkaradvīpa, along with their respective mountains and rivers. It also describes the Lokāloka mountain and the golden land beyond.

beats.title: Description of the Netherworlds and Hells
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the seven netherworlds (pātālas) and various hells (narakas) located in the Puṣkaradvīpa, along with the cosmic structure of the universe, including the egg of Brahmā surrounded by elements and Nārāyaṇa.

beats.title: Description of the Celestial Chariots
beats.summary: This chapter describes the celestial chariots of the Sun, Moon, planets, and other celestial bodies, detailing their dimensions, horses, and the deities associated with each month. It also lists the sages, gandharvas, apsaras, and nagas that accompany the Sun.

beats.title: Nakṣatra, Tithi, and Yoga for Auspicious Timing
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana enumerates the 27 nakshatras (lunar mansions) with their presiding deities, classifies them by direction (adhovakrā, pārśvamukhā, ūrdhvamukhā) and suitability for various activities, lists auspicious and inauspicious tithis (lunar days) and their planetary lords, describes dagdha (burnt) tithis and nakshatra-planet combinations, and outlines amṛta (nectar) and viṣa (poison) yogas for determining favorable times for actions like travel, construction, and ceremonies.

beats.title: Astrological Teachings on Planetary Periods and Omens
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses astrological topics including the durations and effects of planetary periods (dashas) for the Sun, Moon, Mars, Mercury, Jupiter, Venus, Saturn, and Rahu; the zodiac signs ruled by each planet; auspicious and inauspicious omens for travel; and the placement of lunar mansions (nakshatras) on the human body to determine a person's characteristics and fate.

beats.title: The Twelve Phases of the Moon and Auspicious Planetary Positions
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the twelve phases (avasthas) of the moon and their effects, as well as auspicious and inauspicious planetary positions for various activities. It also covers the classification of nakshatras (lunar mansions) for travel and ceremonies, and the compatibility of zodiac signs.

beats.title: Characteristics of Men and Women
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics of men and women based on physical features such as feet, legs, genitals, urine, belly, forehead lines, and palm lines, correlating them with wealth, lifespan, and social status.

beats.title: Lakshanas of Women
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the auspicious and inauspicious physical characteristics of women, including hair, face, eyes, lips, palms, feet, and other body parts, and their implications for marital happiness, wealth, and social status.

beats.title: Lakshana Shastra: Bodily Signs of Fortune
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the auspicious and inauspicious bodily characteristics (laksanas) of men and women, particularly focusing on feet, legs, thighs, genitals, urine, hips, abdomen, navel, chest, neck, arms, hands, fingers, nails, and palm lines. It correlates these features with wealth, longevity, progeny, and social status.

beats.title: Lakshanas: Physical Signs of Fortune and Character
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 65, part 2) describes physical characteristics (lakshanas) of men and women, correlating them with fortune, lifespan, and moral qualities. It covers teeth, lips, chin, beard, ears, nose, eyes, eyebrows, forehead, hair, and other body parts, along with auspicious marks on hands and feet. The text also lists specific features for kings, wealthy individuals, and those destined for misfortune.

beats.title: Samudrika Shastra: Signs of Women and Men
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 65, part 3) describes the science of palmistry and physiognomy (Samudrika Shastra) for women and men. It details various lines on the hand, such as those from the wrist to the middle finger and palm, and interprets their implications for longevity, wealth, and happiness. It also covers bodily features like fingers not touching the ground, leg shapes, hairiness, navel, neck, eyes, cheeks, forehead, lips, breasts, ears, teeth, and hands, associating them with character traits such as promiscuity, widowhood, poverty, or auspiciousness. The chapter concludes by stating that these signs apply to both sexes.

beats.title: Worship of Sudarshana and Shalagrama, and Astrological Calculations
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the worship of the Sudarshana chakra and Shalagrama shila, the classification of divine forms (vyuhas), sacred places (tirthas), the sixty-year cycle of Jupiter (samvatsaras), and a method for determining auspicious and inauspicious times based on vowels and a five-fire diagram. It also includes a mantra for protection.

beats.title: Svarodaya: The Science of Breath and Auspicious Timing
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 67) discusses the science of svarodaya (the rising of breath) and the three main nadis (ida, pingala, and sushumna) within the body. It explains how the flow of breath through these nadis determines auspicious and inauspicious times for various activities such as travel, battle, marriage, and rituals. The text correlates the nadis with the sun, moon, and fire, and provides guidance on interpreting breath patterns for success or failure in endeavors.

beats.title: The Origin and Examination of Gems
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origin of gems from the body of the demon Bala, who was sacrificed by the gods. It then provides a detailed classification, examination, and valuation of diamonds (vajra) and other precious stones, including their colors, qualities, defects, and suitability for different castes. The chapter emphasizes the importance of proper testing and warns against imitation stones.

beats.title: Muktāphala Parīkṣā: The Examination of Pearls
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origins, characteristics, classification, testing, and valuation of pearls (muktāphala). It lists eight sources of pearls, with oyster-born pearls being the most common, and details the qualities of a good pearl: white, round, lustrous, heavy, and flawless. The chapter also provides methods for cleaning and testing pearls, and states that a perfect pearl brings prosperity and protects from harm.

beats.title: The Examination of Rubies
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin and characteristics of various gemstones, especially padmarāga (ruby), from the river Rāvaṇagaṅgā, which was formed when the sun dropped the blood of a great demon into it. It details the classification of gems by origin, color, luster, and quality, and warns against wearing defective or imitation gems.

beats.title: The Origin and Qualities of Emeralds
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin of emeralds (marakata) from the bile of the demon king (dānavādhipati) that fell from Garuḍa's beak onto a mountain, creating a mine. It details the characteristics, qualities, defects, and uses of emeralds, including their efficacy against poison and their value compared to other gems.

beats.title: Examination of the Indranīla Gem
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics, varieties, and testing methods of the indranīla (sapphire) gemstone. It compares indranīla to padmarāga (ruby) and discusses its qualities, value, and how to distinguish genuine stones from imitations.

beats.title: The Examination of the Vaidūrya Gem
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origin, characteristics, varieties, and evaluation of the vaidūrya (cat's eye) gemstone. It explains how the gem was born from the roar of a demon, its colors, imitations, and methods for testing its quality and value.

beats.title: Examination of the Topaz Gem
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origin and characteristics of the gemstone known as puṣparāga (topaz), which is said to have arisen from the skin of a demon that fell on the Himālaya mountain. It details the various color varieties, their names (padmarāga, kaukaṇṭaka, kāṣāyaka, somālaka, indranīla), and the value and benefits of wearing the gem, particularly for women desiring sons.

beats.title: Examination of Karketana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics, types, and auspiciousness of the gemstone called karketana (a type of ruby or garnet). It explains how the gem is formed, its colors, qualities, and the benefits of wearing it, as well as how to identify inferior stones.

beats.title: Examination of the Bhishma Gem
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the properties, varieties, and benefits of the Bhishma gem (a type of cat's eye gemstone). It explains where it is found, its colors, how to wear it, and the protections it offers against various dangers. It also warns against defective stones.

beats.title: Pulaka Parikshana (Examination of Moles)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the characteristics of auspicious and inauspicious moles (pulaka) on the body, their colors, shapes, and effects. It also mentions the price of a mole.

beats.title: Rudhirākṣa Gem Examination
beats.summary: This chapter describes the characteristics and testing of the rudhirākṣa (blood-eyed) gem, including its color, shape, and properties. It also mentions that such gems are found in certain regions like Kāverī, Vindhya, Yavana, Cīna, and Nepāla.

beats.title: Origin and Properties of Crystal
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin and characteristics of crystal (sphaṭika), which is said to have arisen from the fat of the demon Bala, scattered by Lāṅgalī in various regions. The crystal is pure like the sky, white like lotus fiber or conch, with slight color variations, and is considered unequaled among gems and capable of destroying sins. The chapter also mentions that when properly crafted by a skilled artisan, it yields some value.

beats.title: Examination of Coral and Other Gems
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the origin and characteristics of coral (vidruma), stating that it was created from the entrails of the demon Bala thrown by Śeṣa. It details the best varieties of coral, their colors, and their auspicious properties, including bringing wealth, grains, and relief from poison and fear. The chapter also mentions the examination of other gems like ruby and crystal.

beats.title: The Glories of Sacred Tirthas
beats.summary: Suta narrates the glories of various sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites), listing many geographical locations and rivers, and emphasizes that internal tirthas like mind-control and self-restraint are supreme. The chapter concludes with the declaration that Gayā is the best of all tirthas, granting liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Gaya
beats.summary: Brahma narrates the glory of Gaya, describing how the demon Gaya was slain by Vishnu, the establishment of the holy site, and the immense merit of performing shraddha and pinda-dana there, which grants liberation.

beats.title: The Sacred Geography of Gayā and the Benefits of Ancestral Rites
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacred geography of Gayā, listing numerous tīrthas (holy sites) and the benefits of performing śrāddha, piṇḍadāna, and darśana there. It emphasizes that Gayā is the most sacred place for ancestor rituals, and that offerings made there become akṣaya (imperishable). The chapter also mentions specific deities and locations, such as Janārdana, Phalgutīrtha, and Akṣayavaṭa, and the liberation of ancestors from the cycle of rebirth.

beats.title: Gayā Māhātmya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacredness of Gayā and various tīrthas (holy sites) for performing śrāddha (ancestral rites) and piṇḍadāna (offering of rice balls). It emphasizes that offerings made in Gayā, especially during eclipses or at specific locations like the Vaitaraṇī river, can liberate ancestors from hell and grant them heavenly realms. The chapter lists numerous tīrthas such as Rāmatīrtha, Mataṅgavāpī, and Somakuṇḍa, each with specific benefits. It also highlights the importance of feeding brāhmaṇas and releasing bulls (vṛṣotsarga) in Gayā.

beats.title: The Glory of Gaya: Shraddha Rituals and Liberation of Ancestors
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the pilgrimage to Gaya and the rituals of shraddha (offerings to ancestors) performed there. It details various tirthas (sacred sites) within Gaya, the procedures for offering pindas (rice balls) to ancestors, and the benefits of these acts, including liberation of ancestors from hell and attainment of heaven. The chapter also includes a story of a merchant who performed shraddha for a pret (ghost) and thereby liberated his own ancestors.

beats.title: The Pinda Offering at Gayā for Ancestral Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ritual of offering pindas (rice balls) to ancestors at Gayā, specifically at the Pretaśilā. It lists various categories of deceased beings—including those who died by suicide, accident, or in hells—and prescribes mantras and offerings for their liberation. The chapter emphasizes the importance of performing these rites to free oneself from the three debts (ṛṇatraya).

beats.title: The Glory of Pretashila in Gaya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacred Pretashila (stone for the dead) in Gaya, its divine nature as a manifestation of all deities, and the benefits of performing rituals like shraddha and pindadana there. It lists various deities and their associated boons, emphasizing that worship of Adigadadhara (Vishnu) grants all desires and liberation. The chapter also recounts the story of Gayasura and the origin of the hill and stone.

beats.title: The Fourteen Manvantaras
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates the fourteen Manus, their sons, the seven sages, the groups of deities, and the Indras for each Manvantara, along with the avatars of Vishnu who slay the corresponding demons.

beats.title: Enumeration of Manvantaras and Avatars
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana lists the sons of various Manus, the sages, and the gods of each Manvantara, along with the avatars of Vishnu who slay the chief demons. It also enumerates the eighteen vidyas (branches of knowledge) established by Vyasa.

beats.title: The Dialogue Between Ruci and the Pitrs
beats.summary: The chapter narrates a dialogue between the sage Ruci and his ancestors (Pitrs). Ruci, a detached ascetic, is questioned by his Pitrs about his refusal to marry and perform householder duties. They argue that marriage and rituals are essential for spiritual progress and to repay debts to gods, ancestors, and beings. Ruci counters that attachment leads to suffering and bondage, advocating for renunciation and knowledge. The Pitrs explain that prescribed actions performed without desire purify the self and lead to liberation, while inaction leads to downfall. Ultimately, the Pitrs disappear, and the sage Markandeya recounts this story to Krauncuki.

beats.title: Ruchi's Penance and Hymn to the Pitrs
beats.summary: The sage Ruchi, desiring a wife to fulfill his father's command, performs penance to Brahma. Brahma appears and tells him he will become a Prajapati and should propitiate the Pitrs (ancestors) through offerings. Ruchi then performs a tarpaṇa (offering) to the Pitrs on a riverbank and recites a hymn praising them, describing their various forms and the offerings they receive from different beings. The Pitrs appear as a mass of light, and Ruchi continues his hymn.

beats.title: Ruci's Hymn to the Pitrs
beats.summary: The sage Ruci praises the Pitrs (ancestors) with a hymn, seeking a wife and progeny. The Pitrs appear, grant his wish, and declare that reciting this hymn during Shraddha rituals ensures their eternal satisfaction, even if the ritual is flawed.

beats.title: The Birth of Ruci's Son through Māninī
beats.summary: Mārkaṇḍeya narrates how the apsaras Pramlocā emerged from a river and approached the sage Ruci, offering him a beautiful maiden named Māninī, born from Varuṇa's son Puṣkara. Ruci accepted her as his wife, and from their union a mighty son was born.

beats.title: Meditation on Hari
beats.summary: Suta narrates the meditation on Hari (Vishnu) as described by the sages. The meditation focuses on the formless, attributeless, supreme Brahman, beyond the five elements, senses, mind, intellect, and ego. It describes the supreme self as devoid of all dualities, the witness of the three states (waking, dream, deep sleep), and the fourth (turiya). The chapter concludes that one who meditates thus becomes Brahman.

beats.title: Meditation on Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the meditation (dhyāna) on Lord Vishnu, detailing his divine form, attributes, and the benefits of such meditation. It emphasizes that meditating on Vishnu leads to liberation and cites the sage Yājñavalkya as an example.

beats.title: Yājñavalkya's Discourse on Varṇa Dharma
beats.summary: Yājñavalkya, having meditated on Viṣṇu, explains to the sages in Mithilā the essence of dharma, including the fourteen seats of knowledge, the dharmaśāstra authors, the definition of dharma as charity to a worthy recipient, the supreme dharma of self-realization through yoga, the four varṇas, and the saṃskāras from conception to cremation.

beats.title: Yājñavalkya on the Duties of the Twice-Born
beats.summary: Yājñavalkya describes the rules for the upanayana (sacred thread ceremony) and the duties of a brahmacārin (celibate student), including timing of initiation, daily rituals (sandhyā, agnikārya), begging for alms, study of Vedas, and the importance of the guru. The chapter emphasizes that the Veda is the supreme means to liberation for the twice-born.

beats.title: Householder Duties
beats.summary: Yajnavalkya instructs sages on the duties of a householder, including marriage rules, types of marriages, treatment of wives, and conduct of women.

beats.title: Duties of Householders and Mixed Castes
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, narrated by Yajnavalkya, details the duties and rules for householders (grihastha), including mixed caste origins, daily rituals, hospitality, and prohibitions. It emphasizes dharma for all varnas and the importance of self-discipline.

beats.title: Ācāra and Food Prohibitions
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 96, part 2) provides detailed instructions on proper conduct (ācāra), including prohibitions against stepping on certain substances, rules for bathing, types of food to avoid, and expiations for eating forbidden items. It also lists classes of people from whom food should not be accepted and prescribes penances for violations.

beats.title: Purification of Materials and Ācamana Rules
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the purification of various materials and objects used in rituals, including metals, wood, cloth, and food, as well as rules for sipping water (ācamana) after certain actions. It also introduces the next chapter on the rules of giving gifts (dāna).

beats.title: The Merits of Charity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Yajnavalkya, details the rules and merits of charity (dana), especially the gift of a cow (godana), and the importance of giving to worthy recipients. It also discusses the sin of accepting gifts from unworthy persons and the merit of copying scriptures.

beats.title: Śrāddha Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, attributed to Yajnavalkya, provides a detailed manual for performing Śrāddha rituals to honor ancestors. It specifies auspicious times, eligible and ineligible Brahmins, step-by-step procedures including offerings, mantras, and the handling of piṇḍas, along with the fruits of performing Śrāddha on various lunar days and asterisms.

beats.title: Ritual for Propitiating Vināyaka
beats.summary: Yājñavalkya describes the symptoms of a person afflicted by Vināyaka (Gaṇapati) and prescribes a detailed ritual for propitiation, including bathing, offerings, and worship of Ambikā, to remove obstacles and bestow blessings.

beats.title: Graha Shanti Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the procedure for performing a Graha Shanti (pacification of planets) ritual. It lists the nine planets (Surya, Chandra, Mangala, Budha, Brihaspati, Shukra, Shani, Rahu, Ketu), their associated colors, materials for making images, offerings, mantras, and prescribed donations. The ritual is intended to mitigate the malefic effects of planetary influences.

beats.title: Duties of the Forest-Dweller
beats.summary: Yājñavalkya describes the duties of the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) stage of life, including renunciation, ascetic practices, and meditation.

beats.title: The Dharma of a Sannyasi
beats.summary: Yajnavalkya describes the dharma of a sannyasi (renunciate), including the process of leaving home, taking up the staff and water pot, begging for alms, and the ultimate goal of liberation through yoga.

beats.title: Karmic Consequences and Rebirth
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the karmic consequences of sins, detailing the specific animal or degraded human births that result from various misdeeds such as murder, theft, and adultery. It also explains that after suffering in animal forms, souls may be reborn as virtuous humans in noble families.

beats.title: Prāyaścitta: Atonement for Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana enumerates various sins (mahāpāpas and upapāpas) and prescribes corresponding prāyaścittas (atonements). It details the hells that sinners go to if they do not perform atonement, and provides specific penances for crimes such as brahmahatyā, surāpāna, steya, gurutalpaga, and others. The text emphasizes the importance of performing atonement for purification of the inner self and societal acceptance.

beats.title: Prāyaścitta Viveka (Discernment of Atonement)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, attributed to Yājñavalkya, details various prāyaścitta (atonement) practices including kṛcchra, cāndrāyaṇa, and other penances, emphasizing purification from sins through rituals, fasting, and recitation.

beats.title: Rules of Purification and Conduct in Distress
beats.summary: Yājñavalkya explains the rules of śauca (purification) for the deceased, including funeral rites, periods of impurity (āśauca) for various relations and circumstances, and the duties of a brāhmaṇa in times of distress, such as permissible occupations and items not to be sold.

beats.title: Parashara's Discourse on Dharma
beats.summary: Parashara explains dharma for different varnas and ashramas, including duties, purification rules after birth/death (sutaka), penances for sins, and funeral rites. The chapter emphasizes the importance of rituals, charity in Kali Yuga, and consequences of abandoning a virtuous wife.

beats.title: Nītisāra: The Essence of Statecraft
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents the essence of statecraft (nītisāra) as taught by Bṛhaspati to Indra. It emphasizes the importance of associating with the wise, avoiding the wicked, and understanding the role of time (kāla) in all affairs. It also provides guidelines on choosing a good wife and the qualities of a virtuous spouse, contrasting them with harmful ones.

beats.title: Practical Wisdom for Life
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana offers practical wisdom on life, emphasizing the protection of wealth, family, and self. It discusses when to abandon people and places, how to deal with different types of individuals, the dangers of trusting certain entities, the futility of hoarding wealth, the nature of women, and the importance of learning and discernment.

beats.title: Consequences of Falling from Paths
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the consequences of falling from various paths: if one falls from wealth, they should go on a pilgrimage; if from truth, they should go to Raurava hell; if from yoga, they go to Satyaghṛti; if from kingdom, they go to hunting. It concludes with the colophon marking the end of the chapter.

beats.title: Teachings on Dharma and Practical Wisdom
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a collection of moral and practical teachings on various topics such as the importance of steadfastness, the value of knowledge and virtue over birth, the proper treatment of servants and enemies, and the qualities of a wise person. It emphasizes the transient nature of wealth and the importance of associating with virtuous people.

beats.title: Advice to a King on Righteous Governance
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana provides advice to a king (pārthiva) on righteous governance, emphasizing dharma, protection of subjects, restraint of senses, and the importance of wisdom, effort, and proper treatment of servants. It includes metaphors and warnings about the transient nature of life and wealth.

beats.title: Classification of Servants
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, attributed to Bṛhaspati, provides a detailed classification of servants (bhṛtya) based on their qualities and duties. It describes the characteristics of various officials such as the treasurer, gem expert, army commander, gatekeeper, scribe, messenger, judge, cook, physician, and royal priest. It also warns against employing wicked persons, comparing them to a snake adorned with a gem, and advises the king to appoint wise and virtuous servants for the welfare of cows and Brahmins. The chapter concludes with a note on the king's responsibility for the actions of his servants.

beats.title: Discourse on Karma and Virtue
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the importance of associating with virtuous people, the inevitability of karma, and the futility of lamenting over fate. It emphasizes that one's actions determine their destiny, and that true purity lies in truth, self-control, and compassion.

beats.title: Discourse on Karma and Detachment
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the nature of karma, the relationship between action and its results, the futility of attachment, and the cyclical nature of happiness and suffering. It emphasizes that past actions follow the doer like a calf finds its mother among cows, and that one should not grieve over what is done. It also highlights that lowly people see others' faults while ignoring their own, and that attachment leads to fear and suffering, while detachment brings great happiness. The body is the seat of both pleasure and pain, and everything under another's control is suffering, while self-control is happiness. Happiness and suffering alternate like a wheel.

beats.title: On Friendship, Trust, and Right Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the nature of friendship, enmity, and trust, emphasizing that relationships are based on circumstances. It warns against excessive trust, especially in women, and provides advice on health, conduct, and the avoidance of certain behaviors that lead to ruin. The text also lists actions that bring prosperity or destroy it, and highlights the importance of discretion and self-control.

beats.title: Teachings on Dharma and Family
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana presents a series of moral and practical teachings on family, ethics, and self-discipline. It discusses the qualities of a good son, the importance of forgiveness, the dangers of ingratitude, and the need to guard one's mind. The text also warns against associating with evil persons and emphasizes the value of unity among the weak.

beats.title: Moral Teachings on Life and Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 115) is a collection of moral and practical teachings, emphasizing the importance of avoiding bad company, the transient nature of life, the value of honor and self-reliance, and the need for righteous conduct. It contrasts the fleeting nature of wealth and youth with the permanence of dharma and fame, and warns against vices like greed, deceit, and dependency.

beats.title: Teachings on Life and Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 115) presents a collection of moral and practical teachings on life, relationships, and conduct. It covers topics such as the transience of wealth and power, the importance of self-control, the duties of women, the value of education, and the dangers of greed and attachment. The chapter concludes with a note on the transmission of this wisdom from Vishnu to Shiva to Vyasa.

beats.title: Tithi Worship and Its Fruits
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the fruits of worshiping various deities on specific lunar days (tithis) and weekdays. It prescribes which deity to worship on each tithi to obtain desired results such as wealth, grain, sons, kingdom, and victory. The chapter also mentions worship of planets, constellations, and yogas.

beats.title: Anaṅgatrayodaśīvrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Anaṅgatrayodaśīvrata, a thirteen-day vow dedicated to Śiva, detailing the specific flowers, toothsticks, and offerings for each month from Mārgaśīrṣa to Kārttika, along with the concluding rites and benefits.

beats.title: Akhaṇḍa Dvādaśī Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Akhaṇḍa Dvādaśī vow, its procedure, and the benefits of observing it. The vow involves fasting on the twelfth day of the bright fortnight in the month of Mārgaśīrṣa, worshipping Viṣṇu, and donating a pot filled with five grains to a brāhmaṇa. The devotee prays for the vow to remain unbroken across seven births, and the chapter also mentions other similar vows like the Agastya Arghya vow.

beats.title: The Agastya Arghya Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Agastya Arghya Vrata, a ritual offering to the sage Agastya during the transition of the sun into Virgo, which is said to bestow both worldly enjoyment and liberation. The procedure involves preparing a pot with various items, worshipping an image of Agastya made of kasha flowers, and offering arghya with a specific mantra. The chapter also mentions the exclusion of certain castes from the offering and the donation of the pot to a Brahmin.

beats.title: Rambha Tritiya Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the 'Rambha Tritiya' vow, detailing the specific flowers, food offerings, and toothwood types to be used for worshipping various forms of the goddess (Gauri, Girisuta, Subhadra, Gomati, Vishalakshi, Shri Mukhi, Narayani, Madhavi, Shri, Uttama, Rajaputri, Padmaja) in each month from Margashirsha to Kartika, along with the associated observances and donations.

beats.title: Cāturmāsya Vrata Nirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Cāturmāsya vows, including observances on Ekādaśī, starting from Āṣāḍha full moon, and the benefits of various fasting and dietary practices leading to liberation and attainment of Viṣṇu's abode.

beats.title: The Māsopavāsa Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Māsopavāsa vrata, a month-long fast observed from the eleventh day of the bright fortnight of Āśvina until the awakening of Viṣṇu in Kārtika. It details the rules, including daily worship, avoidance of body oil and perfumes, and the breaking of the fast on the twelfth day with a feast for Brahmins. The chapter emphasizes that even if the devotee becomes unconscious, consuming milk or similar items does not break the vow and grants both enjoyment and liberation.

beats.title: Bhishmapanchaka and Kartika Vows
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Bhishmapanchaka vow and other Kartika observances, detailing rituals such as bathing, worship of Vishnu, fasting, and offerings. It emphasizes the importance of Ekadashi and specific tithis for spiritual merit and liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Shivaratri Vrata
beats.summary: Lord Brahma narrates the glory of the Shivaratri vrata, including the story of a sinful hunter who unknowingly performed the vrata and attained liberation. The chapter details the procedure for observing the vrata, including fasting, worship, and vigil on the night of Krishna Chaturdashi in the month of Magha-Phalguna.

beats.title: Ekadashi Mahatmya
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the rules and importance of observing Ekadashi fast, including which lunar days to combine or avoid, and the story of King Rukmangada who attained liberation through Ekadashi observance.

beats.title: The Arcane Worship for Liberation
beats.summary: Brahma describes the arcane worship (arcana) that leads to liberation and enjoyment. He details the construction of a general mandala, placement of deities and powers, and the sequence of worship including the lotus, energies, and the four Vyuhas.

beats.title: The Glory of Bhima Dvadashi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Bhima Dvadashi, a sacred fast observed on the eleventh day of the bright fortnight in the month of Magha when the sun is in the constellation Shravana. It extols the power of this fast to destroy great sins, including brahminicide, and compares it favorably to holy places and other meritorious acts. The chapter also provides detailed instructions for the ritual worship of Lord Varaha (the boar incarnation of Vishnu) on this day, including the construction of an image, offerings, and the recitation of the Purana. Observing this fast with devotion is said to free one from the three debts and grant all desires.

beats.title: Vrataparibhāṣā: Rules for Vows
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, titled 'Vrataparibhāṣā', defines the rules and observances for various vows (vratas). It details the niyamas (restraints), daily practices, forbidden items during fasting, the ten common dharmas for all vows, the procedure for the Brahmakūrca vow, and the different types of months (cāndra, sāvana, saura, nākṣatra). It also discusses the effects of breaking a vow and the remedies, including fasting and shaving the head.

beats.title: Vratas and Rituals for Various Tithis
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various vratas (vows) and rituals associated with specific tithis (lunar days), including the worship of deities such as Shiva, Parvati, Ganesha, and Nagas. It details the procedures, offerings, and benefits of observing these vows, emphasizing the attainment of worldly desires, health, and liberation.

beats.title: Saptami Vrata Nirupana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals and observances for the Saptami (seventh lunar day) vratas, including Phala Saptami and Vijaya Saptami, with specific rules for bathing, worship of the Sun god, offerings, fasting, and donations to Brahmins.

beats.title: Dūrvāṣṭamī and Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes two vratas (religious observances): Dūrvāṣṭamī and Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī. Dūrvāṣṭamī is observed on the eighth day of the bright fortnight of Bhādrapada, involving worship of Dūrvā (a grass), Sūrya (the Sun), Gaṇeśa, and Śiva. Kṛṣṇāṣṭamī is observed on the eighth day of the dark fortnight when Rohiṇī nakṣatra is present, involving worship of Kṛṣṇa with various names and mantras, and offering arghya to the Moon and Rohiṇī. The chapter emphasizes the power of these observances to destroy sins and grant liberation.

beats.title: Budha Ashtami Vrata and the Story of Kaushika
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Budha Ashtami vrata (Mercury's eighth-day vow), its rules, and a story illustrating its power. The narrative follows a Brahmin named Vira, his wife Rambha, their son Kaushika, and daughter Vijaya. Through observing the vrata, they attain liberation and heavenly rewards.

beats.title: Mahānavamīvrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the rituals and mantras associated with the Mahānavamīvrata, including the Aśokāṣṭamī vow, the worship of Durgā with specific iconography, and the offering of animal sacrifices. It details the benefits of observing these rites on specific lunar days and asterisms.

beats.title: Mahakaushika Mantra and Maha Navami Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the Mahakaushika mantra and the rituals associated with the Maha Navami festival, including the worship of various goddesses and the offering of a bali (sacrifice).

beats.title: Vratas for Specific Tithis
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with specific tithis (lunar days) in the months of Ashvin and Chaitra. It details the procedures, offerings, and benefits of observing Vira Navami, Damanaka Navami, Dig Dashami, and Rishi Ekadashi. The chapter emphasizes the importance of worshiping deities, offering specific items, and reciting mantras to attain health, wealth, victory, and ultimately, liberation or a high spiritual status.

beats.title: The Glory of Vijayā Ekādaśī
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the significance of the Vijayā Ekādaśī, which occurs when the eleventh and twelfth lunar days coincide with the Śravaṇa nakṣatra. It prescribes fasting rules, worship procedures including a specific mantra for offering to Vāmana (an avatar of Viṣṇu), and the benefits of observing this vow.

beats.title: Śravaṇadvādaśīvrata Nirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Śravaṇadvādaśīvrata (the vow of the twelfth lunar day of Śravaṇa month) and its benefits, including the destruction of sins and the attainment of all desires. It also mentions the ritual of giving pots to Brahmins at a riverbank.

beats.title: Tithi Vrata Nirupana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) associated with specific tithis (lunar days), nakshatras (constellations), and days of the week. It details the deities to be worshipped on each tithi, the benefits of fasting and offerings, and concludes with a prayer to Acyuta (Vishnu) for the removal of sins and the attainment of desires.

beats.title: Genealogy of the Solar Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana lists the dynasties of kings descended from Manu, starting from Brahma born from Vishnu's navel, through Daksha, and then the solar dynasty (Suryavansha) including Ikshvaku, Mandhata, Sagara, Bhagiratha, and the Rama lineage, among others.

beats.title: Surya Vamsha Genealogy (continued)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 138, part 2) continues the description of the Surya Vamsha (Solar Dynasty), listing a genealogy of kings from Suparshva to Janaka, with an emphasis on the lineage's connection to yoga.

beats.title: Chapter 139 (part 1)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 139, part 1) narrates the lunar dynasty (Somavaṃśa) lineage, starting from Brahmā born from Nārāyaṇa, through Atri, Soma, Budha, Purūravas, and many subsequent kings and sages, including the Yadu and Vṛṣṇi lines leading to Vasudeva and Kṛṣṇa's ancestors.

beats.title: Chapter 139 (part 2)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 139, part 2) details the genealogies of the lunar dynasty (Candravaṃśa), focusing on the descendants of King Kunti and others, including the births of the Pandavas, Kauravas, Yadavas, and various other royal lineages. It lists the wives and children of Vasudeva (including Krishna and Balarama), the lineage of Pradyumna and Aniruddha, and the dynasties of Turvasu, Druhyu, Anu, and others, culminating in the birth of Karna and his son Vrishasena.

beats.title: Chapter 140
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana presents a detailed genealogy of the Lunar Dynasty (Candravaṃśa), tracing the lineage from Janamejaya through various kings and sages, including the Kauravas and Pandavas, culminating in Parīkṣit and Janamejaya. It lists numerous rulers and their descendants, with cross-references to figures from the Mahabharata.

beats.title: Future Royal Dynasties and Cosmic Dissolution
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana lists the future kings of the Ikshvaku and Brihadratha dynasties, culminating in a discourse on the nature of creation and dissolution, emphasizing that all beings are subject to destruction and urging the practice of dharma to attain Hari.

beats.title: The Ten Avatars and the Power of Pativrata Dharma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ten primary avatars of Vishnu (Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, and Kalki) and their deeds to protect dharma. It also narrates the story of the chaste wife (pativrata) of a leprous Brahmin named Kaushika, who through her devotion prevented the sunrise, and how Anasuya, another pativrata, intervened to restore the sun. The chapter highlights the power of pativrata dharma and compares Sita's devotion to that of Anasuya.

beats.title: Ramayana Summary
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana narrates the story of the Ramayana, from the lineage of Brahma to Rama's return to Ayodhya and his reign. It covers key events: Rama's birth, his killing of Tataka, marriage to Sita, exile, Sita's abduction by Ravana, alliance with Sugriva, Hanuman's journey to Lanka, the war, Ravana's defeat, and Rama's coronation.

beats.title: Harivamsha: The Glories of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana narrates the Harivamsha, focusing on the divine exploits of Krishna, including his birth to Vasudeva and Devaki, his childhood pastimes such as killing Putana, overturning the cart, breaking the Yamala-Arjuna trees, subduing Kaliya, killing Dhenuka, lifting Govardhana, and later slaying Kamsa, Keshi, and other demons. It also mentions his marriages to Rukmini, Satyabhama, and others, his son Pradyumna, grandson Aniruddha, and the battle with Shiva over Bana, as well as the killing of Naraka, Shishupala, and Dvivida. The chapter concludes with Krishna's role as a teacher to Sandipani and his establishment of Ugrasena as king.

beats.title: The Bharata War and Avatars
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana narrates the lineage of the Kuru dynasty leading to the Mahabharata war, summarizing key events: the birth of Bhishma, Dhritarashtra, Pandu, and Vidura; the birth of the Kauravas and Pandavas; the Pandavas' escape from the lacquer house, killing of Baka, marriage to Draupadi, receiving Indraprastha, Arjuna's marriage to Subhadra and acquisition of divine weapons, the Rajasuya sacrifice, the dice game and exile, the war with its major battles (Bhishma, Drona, Karna, Shalya, Duryodhana, and Ashvatthama's night raid), Yudhishthira's rule and Ashvamedha, Parikshit's coronation, the Yadava destruction, and Yudhishthira's ascent to heaven. It concludes with the future Kalki avatar and the merit of hearing the Bharata.

beats.title: Sarvaroganidana: The Etiology of All Diseases
beats.summary: Dhanvantari explains the fivefold diagnosis of diseases (nidana, purvarupa, rupa, upashaya, samprapti) and details the causes of dosha aggravation, including dietary and lifestyle factors, leading to various diseases. The chapter emphasizes that the root cause of all diseases is the vitiation of the three doshas (vata, pitta, kapha) due to improper conduct and sinful actions.

beats.title: Classification of Fevers
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 147) provides a detailed classification and description of fever (jvara) from an Ayurvedic perspective. It discusses the origins, types, symptoms, and prognosis of various fevers, including those caused by imbalances of vata, pitta, kapha, and sannipata (all three doshas). It also covers external causes such as injury, curses, and sorcery. The chapter emphasizes the importance of understanding the nature of fever for proper treatment.

beats.title: Classification of Fevers
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 147) details the classification, etiology, and symptomatology of various types of fevers (jvara) based on the imbalance of doshas (vata, pitta, kapha) and their location in the body's tissues (rasa, rakta, mamsa, medas, asthi, majja, shukra). It describes periodic fevers (quotidian, tertian, quartan) and their characteristics, as well as specific types like haridraka. The text also outlines signs of recovery and mentions the next chapter on raktapitta.

beats.title: Etiology of Raktapitta
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the etiology (nidāna) of raktapitta (bleeding disorders), describing its causes, symptoms, and treatment principles. It explains how aggravated pitta and blood combine, leading to various symptoms like heaviness in the head, anorexia, desire for cold, smoke-like vision, sour taste, vomiting, cough, dyspnea, dizziness, fatigue, and discoloration. The chapter emphasizes the importance of purificatory therapies (virecana and vamana) based on the direction of bleeding and associated doshas.

beats.title: Kāsa Nidāna: The Etiology of Cough
beats.summary: Dhanvantari describes the five types of cough (kāsa) based on the doshas: vāta, pitta, kapha, kṣata (trauma), and kṣaya (consumption). Each type's symptoms, pathogenesis, and prognosis are detailed. The chapter emphasizes the importance of prompt treatment to prevent progression to more serious conditions like consumption.

beats.title: Śvāsa Nidāna (Etiology of Respiratory Disease)
beats.summary: Dhanvantari describes the etiology, symptoms, and prognosis of respiratory diseases (śvāsa), including five types: kṣudra, tamaka, chinna, mahā, and ūrdhva. The chapter details causes such as aggravated cough, dosha imbalance, and external factors, and outlines fatal signs.

beats.title: Hiccup Etiology and Classification
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the diagnosis and classification of hiccups (hikrā), describing various types such as kṣudrā, yamalā, mahatī, and gambhīrā, their causes, symptoms, and prognostic implications. It also briefly introduces the topic of yakṣmaroga (consumption/tuberculosis) as a segue to the next chapter.

beats.title: Etiology and Symptoms of Rajayakshma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the etiology, symptoms, and prognosis of Rajayakshma (tuberculosis), also known as Kshaya or consumption. It describes the causes (excess exertion, suppression of natural urges, depletion of semen/ojas, and improper diet), the pathogenesis involving vitiated vata and pitta, and a detailed list of prodromal and clinical symptoms including fever, cough, hemoptysis, and wasting. It also covers complications, doshic differentiation, and prognostic indicators.

beats.title: Diagnosis of Anorexia
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the diagnosis and classification of anorexia (arocaka) based on doshic imbalances. It describes symptoms for vata, pitta, kapha, and sannipata types, as well as complications from worms and other factors. The chapter ends with a transition to the next topic on heart disease.

beats.title: Diagnosis of Heart Disease and Thirst
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 154) discusses the diagnosis (nidāna) of heart diseases (hṛdroga) and thirst (tṛṣṇā), classifying them according to the three doshas (vāta, pitta, kapha) and their combinations. It describes symptoms, causes, and subtypes, including those caused by worms (kṛmi) and complications (upasarga). The chapter is attributed to Dhanvantari.

beats.title: Madātyaya Nidāna
beats.summary: Dhanvantari describes the causes and symptoms of intoxication (madātyaya) from alcohol, classified by doṣa (vāta, pitta, kapha) and their combinations, including stages of intoxication, complications like mūrchā and saṃnyāsa, and the importance of moderation.

beats.title: Treatise on Hemorrhoids
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, spoken by Dhanvantari to Suśruta, provides a detailed medical treatise on hemorrhoids (arśas). It covers the etiology, classification, symptoms, and complications of the disease, emphasizing the role of vāta, pitta, and kapha doṣas. The text describes the anatomical location, types (dry/moist, congenital/acquired), and associated conditions like grahaṇī (malabsorption). It also outlines the systemic effects and poor prognosis for certain types.

beats.title: Nidāna of Arśas (Hemorrhoids)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the classification and treatment of hemorrhoids (arśas). It describes the causes, symptoms, and types based on dosha imbalance, and emphasizes the need for prompt treatment to prevent complications.

beats.title: Etiology of Diarrhea and Digestive Disorders
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the etiology (nidāna) of diarrhea (atīsāra) and chronic digestive disorders (grahaṇī). It describes causes such as excessive water intake, improper diet, and emotional factors like fear and grief. The text details symptoms based on the three doshas (vāta, pitta, kapha) and distinguishes between āma (undigested) and nirāma (digested) types. It also lists eight major diseases (mahārogas) including urinary disorders, skin diseases, and hemorrhoids.

beats.title: Urinary Disorders and Their Causes
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 158) discusses the causes, symptoms, and types of urinary disorders (mūtrāghāta) and related conditions such as aśmarī (urinary stones), vātabasti, vātakuṇḍalikā, mūtragranthi, mūtraśukra, and others. It describes the role of vāta, pitta, and kapha doshas in these diseases, and provides detailed clinical features.

beats.title: Treatise on Urinary Disorders
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 159) is a medical treatise on urinary disorders (prameha). It enumerates twenty types of prameha classified by dosha (kapha, pitta, vata), describes their symptoms, complications (including ten types of skin lesions), and etiological factors such as diet and lifestyle. The text also discusses prognosis and the progression to diabetes (madhumeha).

beats.title: Dhanvantari's Discourse on Vidradhi and Gulma
