It explains why Vyāsa is named at the end of the chapter, linking it to the protection of the text from demons.

beats.title: The Creation and Hierarchy of Beings
beats.summary: Krishna explains to Garuda the nature of creation, the relationship between the supreme being and individual souls, and the hierarchy of deities. He discusses the concept of reflection (bimba-pratibimba), the role of Lakshmi, and the relative superiority of Vishnu over other gods.

beats.title: The Supreme Lord Hari and the True Knower of the Vedas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Uttarakhaṇḍa, Brahmakāṇḍa, Chapter 2) is a dialogue between Kṛṣṇa and Garuḍa. It emphasizes that true knowledge of the Vedas lies in understanding that Hari (Viṣṇu) is the supreme lord of all deities, including Brahmā and Rudra. It criticizes those who merely recite the Vedas without realizing this, calling them 'brāhmaṇagardabha' (donkey among brāhmaṇas). The chapter includes a hymn of praise to Viṣṇu, invoking him under various names and forms, and concludes with Viṣṇu being pleased by the devotee's prayer.

beats.title: Discourse on the Nature of Vishnu and His Energies
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna explains to Garuda the nature of Vishnu's avatars, the relationship between the supreme being and his energies (Maya, Lakshmi), the concept of paramanu (atom), and the infinite qualities of Vishnu. He emphasizes that all avatars are complete and that the supreme form remains unchanged. The chapter also discusses the proper attitude of a disciple towards the guru and warns against harming the guru.

beats.title: The Five Eternal Distinctions and the Creation of Guṇas
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the nature of the Supreme Being's omnipresence and the five eternal distinctions (pañca bheda) between the Lord, sentient beings, and insentient matter. It describes how the Lord, as Puruṣa, imparts power to Prakṛti and creates the three guṇas.

beats.title: The Nature of the Three Gunas and the Unity of the Divine
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana explains the nature of the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their intermixture during creation and dissolution. It describes how the supreme being Hari (Vishnu) enters into Brahma and Rudra to perform creation and destruction, and emphasizes the unity of Vishnu, Brahma, and Rudra. The dialogue between Garuda and Krishna clarifies the proportional mixing of gunas in different states.

beats.title: Resolution of the Guṇa Proportions in Mahattattva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Uttarakhaṇḍa, Brahmakāṇḍa, Chapter 4) is a dialogue between Śrīkṛṣṇa and Garuḍa, resolving an apparent contradiction about the composition of Mahattattva. It explains how the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) combine in specific proportions to form the body of Brahmā, emphasizing that the differences arise from the relative dominance of guṇas. The teaching concludes that knowledge of this leads to liberation.

beats.title: Cosmic Evolution from Mahat-tattva
beats.summary: This chapter describes the evolution of the universe from the mahat-tattva, through the emergence of ahaṅkāra (ego) in its three forms (sāttvika, rājasa, tāmasa), and the subsequent creation of the sense organs, their presiding deities, and various cosmic beings including Rudras, Ādityas, Vasus, Maruts, Viśvedevas, Manus, and others, all under the influence of Hari (Viṣṇu) and Lakṣmī.

beats.title: The Birth of Tattvābhimānī Deities
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth order and attributes of various deities who preside over the tattvas (elements), including Budha (Mercury), Uṣā, Śanaiścara (Saturn), and Puṣkara. It explains that these deities are born from Svāhā and are abhimānīs (personifications) of the tattvas. Finally, Lord Hari (Viṣṇu) enters these tattvas along with Ramā (Lakṣmī).

beats.title: Hymns of Praise by the Deities
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana describes a series of hymns of praise offered to Lord Vishnu by various deities, including Brahma, Vayu, Sarasvati, Bharati, Shesha, Garuda, Rudra, and Varuni. Each deity humbly acknowledges their inability to fully praise Vishnu's infinite qualities, yet offers heartfelt devotion. The chapter emphasizes the supremacy of Vishnu, the importance of devotion, and the power of chanting his glories to attain liberation.

beats.title: Hierarchy of Devas in Praising Viṣṇu
beats.summary: In this chapter, various goddesses (Vāruṇī, Sauparṇī, Girijā) and Indra praise Viṣṇu, each acknowledging their inability to fully glorify him due to their inferiority compared to the previous speaker. The chapter establishes a hierarchy of beings in their capacity to praise Viṣṇu.

beats.title: Hymns of Praise to Vishnu by Various Deities and Sages
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Chapter 7, part 1) features a series of hymns of praise (stuti) offered to Lord Vishnu (Hari) by various deities and sages, each acknowledging their own limitations and inferiority compared to the divine. The beings include Ahaṅkārika (personified ego), Śacī, Rati, Dakṣa, Bṛhaspati, Aniruddha, Svāyaṃbhuva Manu, Varuṇa, Nārada, Bhṛgu, Agni, Prasūti, Vasiṣṭha, and Marīci. Each speaker humbly confesses their inability to fully comprehend or praise Vishnu's glory, often comparing themselves unfavorably to others. The chapter emphasizes the transcendence of Vishnu and the humility of all created beings.

beats.title: Sages Praise Lord Hari
beats.summary: Sages Marīci, Atri, Aṅgiras, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, Manu Vaivasvata, and Viśvāmitra each praise Lord Hari (Viṣṇu) in turn, acknowledging their inability to fully describe his infinite, transcendent nature. The chapter highlights the supremacy of devotion and the power of chanting the Lord's names, especially at the time of death. It concludes by noting that these sages are considered equal in status.

beats.title: Praise of Viṣṇu by the Winds and Viṣvaksena
beats.summary: Mitra, Tārā, Nirṛti, Prāvahī, and Viṣvaksena praise Lord Viṣṇu, describing the path of devotion and the importance of controlling the mind. The chapter concludes with Viṣvaksena's humility and the listing of the four winds (Parjanya, Mitra, Nirṛti, Prāvahī) and their qualities.

beats.title: Devakṛtaviṣṇustutidevatātāratamyanirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana, titled 'Devakṛtaviṣṇustutidevatātāratamyanirūpaṇa' (Description of the Hierarchy of Deities in the Hymn to Vishnu by the Gods), discusses the hierarchy of divine beings. It classifies various categories of gods, gandharvas, sages, and others according to their origin and spiritual status, distinguishing between 'ajānaja' (born of ignorance) and 'karmaja' (born of action) beings. The chapter also includes a hymn to Vishnu and emphasizes the importance of reciting it for attaining purity, health, and Vishnu's grace.

beats.title: The Cosmic Structure and Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter describes the cosmic creation process, focusing on the structure of the universe with multiple layers (āvaraṇas) around the cosmic egg, the Virajā river that purifies beings for liberation, and the hierarchy of beings (deities, sages, etc.) who attain different levels of realization over vast time scales. It also distinguishes between prākṛta (primordial) and vaikṛta (modified) creation.

beats.title: The Eternal Reality and the Supremacy of Keśava
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana emphasizes the eternal and real nature of the cosmic flow (jagatpravāha), asserting that it is not false. It declares that those who deny this truth are destroyers of all. The text repeatedly affirms the supremacy of Keśava (Viṣṇu) over all deities and scriptures, and warns that those who worship others fall into blinding darkness. The chapter concludes with an introduction to the highest creation (vaikṛta sarga) and a transition to a dialogue between Śrī Kṛṣṇa and Garuḍa.

beats.title: The Investigation of Ignorance
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Uttarakhaṇḍa, Dvitīyāṃśa, Dharmakāṇḍa) is a dialogue between Śrī Kṛṣṇa and Garuḍa, focusing on the nature of ignorance (ajñāna) and its role in the cosmic play. Lakṣmī praises Viṣṇu in the cosmic waters, describing two Prakṛtis: one causing bondage (aja) and the other covering knowledge (parā prakṛti). Viṣṇu awakens, a golden lotus emerges from his navel, and Brahmā is born. Brahmā's apparent ignorance and fear are explained as a deliberate act to please Hari and guide seekers. The chapter emphasizes that ignorance is not real in Brahman but is a tool for liberation.

beats.title: The Nature of Divine Knowledge and Ignorance
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna explains to Garuda the nature of divine knowledge and ignorance. He describes how even Brahma, the creator, does not fully comprehend Vishnu's true form, and how various deities like Lakshmi, Rudra, and others have limited understanding based on their own capacity. Krishna emphasizes that he himself is free from ignorance and performs actions only for Vishnu's pleasure, not for personal desire. The chapter also discusses the concept of ignorance as the absence of direct perception (aparoksha) and clarifies that in the ultimate reality, there is no ignorance for the enlightened.

beats.title: The Hierarchy of Demons and the Importance of Non-Criticism of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the nature of Vishnu's avatars, the dangers of criticizing Vishnu or his forms, and the hierarchy of demons. It emphasizes that those who worship non-self-manifested lingas or neglect Tulasi and Shalagrama are considered asuras. The chapter also lists various demons and their relative degrees of evil, culminating in the identification of Duryodhana as the embodiment of endless suffering.

beats.title: Brahma's Praise of Vishnu
beats.summary: Brahma praises Vishnu as the supreme lord, describing his own inability to comprehend Vishnu's greatness and seeking his mercy. The chapter concludes with Brahma standing with folded hands before Hari.

beats.title: The Creation of the Gods
beats.summary: Lord Krishna instructs Brahma to create the gods in a specific sequence, detailing the order of creation from various parts of Brahma's body and the time intervals between creations. The chapter lists the emergence of deities, sages, and other beings, emphasizing that this knowledge leads to liberation.

beats.title: Devotpatti: Origin of the Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Uttarakhaṇḍa, Brahmakāṇḍa) is a dialogue between Śrī Garuḍa and Śrī Kṛṣṇa, focusing on the origin of the deities (devotpatti). It describes the creation and emergence of various gods and cosmic beings.

beats.title: The Essence and Non-Essence of Offerings
beats.summary: Lord Krishna describes the nature of the Supreme Being as full and complete in every part, and explains the concept of 'sāra' (essence) and 'asāra' (non-essence) in various offerings, foods, and practices. He emphasizes that Viṣṇu accepts only the essence of offerings, and that devotees who offer the essence are blessed. The chapter also lists items and times when offerings become devoid of essence, and stresses the importance of devotion and proper observance of Ekādaśī.

beats.title: Enumeration of Vishnu's Avatars
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates the avatars of Vishnu, describing how the supreme being manifests in various forms for specific cosmic purposes, from creation to destruction of demons, and concludes that Vishnu's avatars are infinite and non-different from him.

beats.title: The Forms of Mahalakshmi and the Manifestations of Vayu
beats.summary: Krishna describes the forms and avatars of Mahalakshmi, the goddess of fortune, and her relationship with Vishnu. He also explains the manifestations of Vayu (the wind god) as various principles such as Pradhana, Dhriti, Smriti, Mukti, Citta, Mahaprabhu, Bala, Yashtri, Rjuryogya, Prajna, Jnana, Guru, Mahadhyata, Vijna, Viraga, and Sukha, emphasizing their roles in devotion and liberation.

beats.title: Vayu and Bharati Avatars
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the nature of Vayu (the wind god) and his avatars, the relationship between deities and demons, the concept of Kali, and the incarnations of Bharati (Saraswati). It describes how Vayu's avatars include Hanuman, Bhima, and others, and how Bharati appears as various figures like Draupadi, Damayanti, and others. The chapter also explains the interplay of happiness and suffering among gods and demons, and the role of Vayu in maintaining cosmic order.

beats.title: The Special Body of Bharati: The Origin of Draupadi
beats.summary: This chapter explains why Bharati (the goddess of speech) was born as Draupadi with a special body shared by multiple goddesses. It narrates the curse of Brahma on the wives of Indra, Yama, Vayu, and the Ashvins for their arrogance, leading to their births as Draupadi. The chapter details how they propitiated Bharati, who merged with them, and how Vayu entered the Pandavas to facilitate union with Draupadi, absolving them of sin.

beats.title: The Avatars of Shesha and Rudra
beats.summary: Krishna describes the origins and avatars of Shesha (Ananta) and Rudra (Shiva), including their incarnations as Lakshmana, Balarama, Durvasa, Ashvatthama, and others, and narrates Rudra's emotional reaction upon seeing Vishnu's divine form.

beats.title: The Weeping of Aurva and the Hierarchy of Bliss
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana describes the story of Aurva (a form of Rudra) who weeps for the deluded souls trapped in worldly desires, and then discusses the hierarchy of bliss (ānanda) among various deities, culminating in the supreme bliss of Krishna.

beats.title: The Gradation of Divine Attributes and the Penance of Kavyavaha's Daughter
beats.summary: Garuda asks Krishna about the gradation of divine attributes, particularly bliss and knowledge, among deities. Krishna explains that each being perceives him according to their capacity, and describes the hierarchy of knowledge. He then narrates the story of a maiden (Kavyavaha's daughter) who refuses all suitors, declaring Krishna as her only husband, and performs penance at Shrinivasa's abode.

beats.title: Nīlā's Marriage to Viṣṇu
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Nīlā, a devotee who prays to Lord Śrīnivāsa (Viṣṇu) to become his wife. Viṣṇu promises to marry her in his Kṛṣṇa incarnation. Nīlā is born as the daughter of Kumbhaka, later as Nāgnijit's daughter, and eventually marries Kṛṣṇa after he defeats seven bulls in her svayamvara.

beats.title: Bhadra and Mitravinda: The Power of Devotion
beats.summary: Krishna narrates the stories of Bhadra and Mitravinda, who in previous births performed devotion to Vishnu through namaskara, pradakshina, and listening to Puranas, and thereby became his wives. The chapter emphasizes the supremacy of devotion over rituals and warns against hypocrisy.

beats.title: The Penance and Marriage of Kalindi
beats.summary: Krishna narrates the origin of Kalindi (Yamuna), daughter of Vivasvan (the Sun), who performed severe penance to become Krishna's wife. He describes her anutapa (repentance) for past neglect of devotion, culminating in her realization of Hari's supremacy. Krishna then recounts how he married her on the banks of the Yamuna after sending Arjuna to inquire about her penance.

beats.title: The Auspicious Marks of Viṣṇu and Other Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the 32 auspicious marks (lakṣaṇas) of Lord Viṣṇu and their counterparts in other deities and beings. It explains how these marks are distributed among various gods, goddesses, and celestial beings, and also lists inauspicious marks. The chapter emphasizes the uniqueness of Viṣṇu's marks and warns against comparing them with others.

beats.title: Lakshmana's Marriage to Hari and Krishna's Marriage to Jambavati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the association of various deities with different locations and beings, emphasizing that Vishnu (Hari) resides in all forms and places. It narrates how Lakshmana, by understanding this, pleased Hari and eventually became his wife, and also recounts Krishna's marriage to Jambavati.

beats.title: The Pilgrimage of Jambavati
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Jambavati, the daughter of Soma (the moon god), who in a previous birth was a devoted wife of Krishna. She performed intense devotional practices, including pilgrimage to Venkatesa hill, with strict rules about pilgrimage conduct. The chapter emphasizes the importance of devotion, proper pilgrimage etiquette, and the power of Vishnu's grace.

beats.title: The Glory of Venkata Hill and the Devotees of Srinivasa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the glory of the Venkata hill (Tirumala) and the importance of chanting the names of Lord Srinivasa (Venkateswara). It lists various devotees of the Lord, including sages, kings, rivers, and deities, and emphasizes that true devotion involves chanting His names with love and performing pilgrimage with faith. The chapter also warns against false devotees and explains the nature of true bhakti.

beats.title: The Glory of Nārasiṃha Tīrtha and the Worship of Śrīnivāsa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacred Nārasiṃha tīrtha, where Lord Viṣṇu as Narasiṃha appeared to Prahlāda and the demon children. It details the story of Prahlāda's devotion, the Lord's manifestation in water, and the ritual procedures for worship at the Śrīnivāsa temple, including the names of deities to be honored in various directions and the steps for darshan.

beats.title: Ritual of Salutations on Śrī Veṅkaṭeśa Mountain
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the ritual of offering salutations (namaskara) to various deities in different directions during a pilgrimage to Śrī Veṅkaṭeśa mountain. It details the names of Viṣṇu's forms and associated deities to be worshipped in each direction, emphasizing the secrecy of the ritual. The chapter concludes with the goddess (Śrī) and her companions proceeding to see Śrīnivāsa, and a discourse on the rarity of association with the wise.

beats.title: The Vision and Prayer of the Devotee at Svāmipuṣkariṇī
beats.summary: A devotee (kanyā) visits the Svāmipuṣkariṇī lake near Śrīnivāsa's temple, performs rituals, and has a vision of Lord Viṣṇu (Śrīnivāsa). She offers a heartfelt prayer, receives a mantra from the Lord, and later learns its meaning from sage Jaigiṣavya. The chapter includes a morning prayer (suprabhātam) invoking various forms of Viṣṇu and sacred places.

beats.title: Devī's Bath and Prayer at Venkatesha's Lake
beats.summary: This chapter lists sacred rivers, sages, kings, and divine beings invoked for a blessed dawn. It describes the goddess performing purification and bathing in the Pushkarini lake of Venkatesha, accompanied by sage Jaigishavya, and reciting the mantra of Venkatesha.

beats.title: The Glory of Vyaṅkaṭādri and the Story of Hiraṇyākṣa
beats.summary: Jaigīṣavya narrates the story of the demon Hiraṇyākṣa, his mother Diti, and his eventual slaying by Viṣṇu as Varāha. The chapter describes the origin of the Vyaṅkaṭa hill (Tirumala), the glory of Śrīnivāsa (Viṣṇu), and the sacredness of the Svāmipuṣkariṇī lake and Vāyutīrtha. It emphasizes devotion, the power of holy places, and the importance of satsaṅga (good company) for liberation.

beats.title: The Sacred Tīrthas and Śālagrāma Śilās of Śrīnivāsa
beats.summary: This chapter describes the various tirthas (sacred waters) around the Śrīnivāsa temple, including Vāyutīrtha, Candratīrtha, Raudratīrtha, Brahmātīrtha, Indratīrtha, and Vahnitīrtha. It details the unique Śālagrāma śilā (sacred stones) associated with each tīrtha, such as Dadhivāmana, Bhūvarāha, Lakṣmīnṛsiṃha, Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa, Sītārāma, and Vāsudeva, and emphasizes the rarity and great merit of bathing, donating, and hearing about these forms, especially in the Kali age.

beats.title: The Sacred Tirthas of Venkata Hill and the Glories of Shalagrama Donation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana describes the sacred geography of the Venkata hill, focusing on the tirthas (holy waters) surrounding the shrine of Srinivasa. It details the characteristics and benefits of bathing in and donating Shalagrama stones at each tirtha, including Yama, Nirriti, Shesha, Varuna, and Svamipushkarini. The chapter emphasizes the rarity and great merit of such acts, especially in the Kali Yuga, and concludes with the story of a devout woman who performed these rites.

beats.title: The Pilgrimage of the Kanyā and the Glory of Veṅkaṭādri
beats.summary: The chapter describes a sage Jaigīṣavya guiding a woman (a kanyā, later identified as Jāmbavatī) through a pilgrimage of various sacred tīrthas (holy waters) on the Veṅkaṭādri hill, culminating in the worship of Śrīnivāsa (Viṣṇu). The narrative includes the purifying power of these tīrthas, the story of the woman's past life as Satī, and instructions on the proper offering of naivedya to Śrīnivāsa.

beats.title: Avatars and Identities of Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana discusses the avatars and identities of various deities and beings, tracing their origins and relationships. It explains how Sati became Parvati, the multiple forms of Varuni, and the incarnations of Indra, Kama, and other gods in different yugas, including their roles in the Ramayana and Mahabharata.

beats.title: The Two Wives (Buddhi) and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: Krishna explains the nature of the two wives (buddhi) – the inferior one leads to delusion and false worship, while the superior one leads to liberation. He describes various misguided practices and emphasizes the supremacy of Vishnu.

beats.title: The Futility of Worshipping Minor Deities and the Supremacy of Viṣṇu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana (Uttarakhaṇḍa, Brahmakāṇḍa, chapter 28) is a discourse on the futility of worshipping minor deities and the supreme importance of devotion to Viṣṇu. It repeatedly warns against various superstitious practices and emphasizes that only Viṣṇu can grant liberation. The text uses vivid imagery of Yama's servants punishing those who rely on false gods, and concludes by urging the seeker to take refuge in Viṣṇu and the true guru.

beats.title: Origins and Daily Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter of the Garuda Purana discusses the origins and identities of various deities and beings, including Dharma, Yama, Gaṅgā, and others, tracing their relationships and incarnations. It also prescribes daily practices for remembering Viṣṇu at various activities, emphasizing devotion and purity.

beats.title: Tattvarahasya: The Secret of Reality
beats.summary: This chapter from the Garuda Purana prescribes specific names and forms of Vishnu to be remembered during various daily activities such as eating, bathing, dressing, sleeping, and other rituals. It emphasizes that all actions should be performed with mindfulness of Hari, making them pleasing to Him. The chapter concludes by stating that hearing this teaching with faith brings devotion to Hari.
